censoredCI 0.1.0
  • Reference
  • Articles
    • getting-started-with-censoredCI
  • Changelog

Inference for Type-II Censoring Schemes

Source: R/ttcs.R
ttcs.Rd

ttcs Computes the Maximum Likelihood Estimates (MLE), variances, standard errors, and asymptotic 95\ of any user-defined lifetime distribution under a Type-II Censoring scheme.

ttcs(
  x,
  par_start,
  pdf_f,
  cdf_f,
  r1,
  r2,
  censoring = "double",
  optimizer = "auto"
)

Arguments

x

A numeric vector containing the observed data points.

par_start

A numeric vector indicating the initial starting values for the parameters.

pdf_f

A user-defined R function that represents the analytical Probability Density Function (PDF).

cdf_f

A user-defined R function that represents the analytical Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF).

r1

An integer specifying the number of left-censored observations (set to 0 if not left-censored).

r2

An integer specifying the number of right-censored observations (set to 0 if not right-censored).

censoring

A character string defining the censoring architecture. Must be one of "left", "right", or "double". Default is "double".

optimizer

A character string selecting the optimization strategy. If set to "auto", all available global metaheuristics are evaluated. Default is "auto".

Value

A list containing three distinct components:

  • estimates: A data frame displaying the estimated parameters, their variances, standard errors, 95\

  • stats: A named numeric vector containing the maximized Log-Likelihood value (LogLik), Akaike Information Criterion (AIC), and Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC).

  • optimizer: A character string stating the name of the best-performing optimization algorithm.

Details

The parameters are estimated by maximizing the censored log-likelihood function. To guarantee that the scale and shape parameters remain strictly positive during numerical optimization, the internal objective function optimizes the parameters on the log-scale and maps them back using the exponential function. Standard errors are derived from the numerical Hessian matrix evaluated at the joint MLEs.

References

Aarset, M. V. (1987). How to identify a bathtub hazard rate. IEEE Transactions on Reliability, 36(1), 106-108.

Murthy, D. P., Xie, M., & Jiang, R. (2004). Weibull models. John Wiley & Sons.

Ijaz, M., Mashwani, W. K., & Belhaouari, S. B. (2020). An innovative family in the lifetime distribution field with applications to real and simulated data. PLOS ONE, 15(10), e0238746.

Examples

# Example using a standard two-parameter Weibull distribution
my_data <- c(0.040, 1.866, 2.385, 3.443, 0.301, 1.876, 2.481, 3.467, 0.309, 1.899,
             2.610, 3.478, 0.557, 1.911, 2.625, 3.578, 0.943, 1.912, 2.632, 3.595,
             1.070, 1.914, 2.646, 3.699, 1.124, 1.981, 2.661, 3.779, 1.248, 2.010,
             2.688, 3.924, 1.281, 2.038, 2.823, 4.035, 1.281, 2.085, 2.890, 4.121,
             1.303, 2.089, 2.902, 4.167, 1.432, 2.097, 2.934, 4.240, 1.480, 2.135,
             2.962, 4.255, 1.505, 2.154, 2.964, 4.278, 1.506, 2.190, 3.000, 4.305,
             1.568, 2.194, 3.103, 4.376, 1.615, 2.223, 3.114, 4.449, 1.619, 2.224,
             3.117, 4.485, 1.652, 2.229, 3.166, 4.570, 1.652, 2.300, 3.344, 4.602,
             1.757, 2.324, 3.376, 4.663)

# Define PDF and CDF in the User Section
user_pdf <- function(x, par) {
   (par[2]/par[1]) * (x/par[1])^(par[2]-1) * exp(-(x/par[1])^par[2])
}
user_cdf <- function(x, par) {
   1 - exp(-(x/par[1])^par[2])
}

# Run the Type-II Censoring Inference
result <- ttcs(x = my_data,
                 par_start =  c(.2,.2),
                 pdf_f = user_pdf,
                 cdf_f = user_cdf,
                 r1 = 1,
                 r2 = 1,
                 censoring = "double",
                 optimizer = "auto")
#> 
#> ========================================
#>    ALL METHODS COMPARISON
#> ========================================
#>   Method      NLL
#> 1     GA 129.7083
#> 2     DE 129.7083
#> 3    PSO 129.7083
#> ----------------------------------------
#> >>> Best method: DE | NLL: 129.7083 
#> ========================================
#> 

print(result$estimates)
#>      Estimate   Variance  StdError CI_Lower CI_Upper Selected_Method    LogLik
#> par1 2.883754 0.01871885 0.1368169 2.615593 3.151915              DE -129.7083
#> par2 2.416434 0.04613668 0.2147945 1.995436 2.837431              DE -129.7083
